sections.header: Verses 1–8 — "Hand This Man Over to Satan"

heb.paragraph: The noun porneia (sexual immorality) is a broad term encompassing all illicit sexual activity. In this case, the specific act is that a man ‘has his father’s wife’ (v. 1) — a euphemism for an ongoing sexual relationship with his stepmother. Such conduct was prohibited by both the Torah (Lev 18:8; Deut 22:30) and Roman law (the lex Iulia de adulteriis). Paul’s outrage is intensified by the fact that even Gentile moral standards (ethne) condemned such behaviour. The term porneia becomes a key word in the letter, recurring at 5:1; 6:13, 18; 7:2; 10:8.

The noun zymē (leaven/yeast) in vv. 6–8 draws on the Passover tradition of removing all leaven from the household (Exod 12:15). Paul deploys the metaphor: a small amount of yeast works through the whole batch of dough. Tolerating the incestuous man’s sin will corrupt the entire community. The command to ‘get rid of the old yeast’ (v. 7) and the identification of Christ as ‘our Passover lamb’ (to pascha hēmōn, v. 7) ground the ethical imperative in Christology: because Christ has been sacrificed, the community must live in purity.

refs.note: The Torah explicitly prohibits uncovering the nakedness of one’s father’s wife — the offence Paul addresses here.
Jesus’ instructions on church discipline provide the dominical precedent for the communal judgment Paul commands.
Paul mentions handing Hymenaeus and Alexander over to Satan ‘to be taught not to blaspheme’ — a parallel remedial use of excommunication.

echoes.source_context: The Passover lamb was to be without defect, its blood applied for protection, its flesh eaten by the household. It was a substitutionary sacrifice — the lamb died so the firstborn would live.
echoes.connection: Paul declares: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.' The imperative to cleanse out the old leaven connects the cross to the feast's call for purity and readiness.
echoes.significance: Paul makes explicit what the Gospels narrate: Jesus' death at Passover was not coincidental but typological fulfillment. Every Passover anticipated the true Lamb.

notes.note: The sexual sin is shocking, but Paul is equally appalled by the church’s arrogance (pephysiōmenoi, ‘puffed up’) about it. They should have mourned (penthein, the word used for mourning the dead). The church’s tolerance of the sin is a corporate failure as serious as the individual sin itself.

Paul pronounces judgment in absentia, assembled ‘in spirit’ with the congregation, invoking ‘the power of our Lord Jesus.’ The phrase ‘hand this man over to Satan’ means formal excommunication. The purpose is redemptive: the destruction of the flesh (sarx) aims at the salvation of his spirit (pneuma) on the day of the Lord.

The Passover metaphor is the theological heart of the passage. Christ is our Passover lamb — already sacrificed. Therefore the community must celebrate the ongoing festival of the Christian life with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth, not the old leaven of malice and wickedness.

Calvin warns that tolerance of notorious sin under the guise of charity is in fact cruelty — cruelty to the sinner (who is confirmed in sin), to the church (which is corrupted), and to Christ (whose name is dishonoured).

Calvin interprets ‘destruction of the flesh’ as the mortification of sinful desires through the discipline of suffering, not physical death. The goal is always redemptive: Satan is unwittingly enlisted in God’s saving purpose. Calvin emphasises that the Passover imagery makes sanctity a christological imperative, not merely a moral preference.
The phrase holōs akouetai (‘it is actually reported’) uses holōs as an intensifier: ‘it is widely/actually heard.’ The report has reached Paul in Ephesus, indicating the scandal was well known.

The phrase eis olethron tēs sarkos (‘for the destruction of the flesh’) is debated: sarx may mean the physical body (implying illness or death) or the sinful nature (implying moral transformation through suffering). Most commentators favour the latter.

Fee argues that the Corinthians’ arrogant tolerance reflects their over-realised eschatology: if they have already entered the spiritual kingdom, bodily behaviour becomes irrelevant. Paul’s response is that the body matters because Christ was raised bodily (ch. 15) and the body is a temple of the Spirit (6:19).

Fee notes that the Passover metaphor presupposes a community already celebrating the ‘festival’ of the new covenant inaugurated by Christ’s sacrifice. The ethical imperative flows from the indicative: because Christ our Passover has been sacrificed, therefore cleanse out the old leaven.

Thiselton emphasises that Paul’s judgment is not unilateral but corporate: it is to be carried out ‘when you are assembled’ (synachthentōn hymōn) with Paul’s apostolic authority backing the congregation’s decision. Church discipline is an ecclesial act, not an individual decree.

Thiselton connects the leaven metaphor to the broader Pauline pattern of indicative-imperative: the indicative (‘Christ our Passover has been sacrificed’) grounds the imperative (‘get rid of the old leaven’). The order is theologically non-negotiable: action follows from identity, not the reverse.

hist.context: Paul now turns from the general problem of divisions (chs. 1–4) to specific moral failures. The case involves a man cohabiting with his stepmother — probably his father’s wife after the father’s death or divorce. Both Leviticus 18:8 and Roman law (the lex Iulia) prohibited such unions. The Corinthian church’s response was not grief but arrogance (v. 2): they may have interpreted their tolerance as evidence of spiritual maturity or ‘freedom’ in Christ. Paul’s instruction to ‘hand this man over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh’ (v. 5) is one of the most debated phrases in the letter. It likely refers to excommunication — removal from the protective sphere of the church into the domain of Satan — with the hope that physical or social consequences will produce repentance and ultimate salvation.

heb.paragraph: The verb krinō (to judge) appears five times in vv. 12–13, creating a concentrated meditation on the proper scope of Christian judgment. Paul distinguishes between judging outsiders (which is God’s prerogative) and judging insiders (which is the church’s responsibility). The distinction directly contradicts the Corinthians’ practice: they were judging the world through lawsuits (6:1–8) while failing to judge the sinner within their own community. Paul’s quotation of Deut 17:7 / 19:19 / 22:21 (‘Expel the wicked person from among you’) roots ecclesial discipline in Torah.

refs.note: The direct source of Paul’s concluding command — the formula used in Israel’s covenant community to execute judgment on serious offenders.
Jesus’ instruction to treat the unrepentant sinner as ‘a pagan or a tax collector’ parallels Paul’s call for exclusion of the persistently immoral.
Paul instructs the Thessalonians to ‘take special note’ of the disobedient and not associate with them, while still warning them as brothers.

notes.note: Paul corrects a misreading of his earlier letter. Total separation from sinners is impossible in a pagan city. What is possible and necessary is refusing fellowship with a professing believer who lives in unrepentant sin. The vice list covers both sexual and economic sins: greed, swindling, and drunkenness are treated as seriously as sexual immorality.

Paul draws a sharp line: the church judges its own members; God judges outsiders. This is not an abdication of the church’s mission to the world but a recognition that moral accountability operates within the covenant community. The Deuteronomy quotation establishes that expulsion is a covenant act, not a social preference.

Calvin notes that Paul’s distinction between the world’s sinners and the church’s sinners is practical wisdom: the church cannot impose its standards on unbelievers, but it must maintain discipline among those who claim Christ’s name. The refusal to eat with such a person (v. 11) likely includes exclusion from the Lord’s Supper.

Calvin reads the Deuteronomy quotation as evidence that the NT church inherits Israel’s responsibility for communal holiness. Discipline is not optional but a mark of a true church. Calvin warns that a church that neglects discipline will inevitably decline into corruption.

The phrase en tē epistolē (‘in my letter’) refers to a previous letter, now lost, sometimes called the ‘previous letter’ or ‘Letter A.’ This confirms that 1 Corinthians is not the first communication between Paul and Corinth.
The instruction mēde synesthiein (‘not even to eat with such a person’) likely refers to shared meals, including the Lord’s Supper, in the house-church setting.

Fee stresses that Paul’s vice list targets both horizontal (social-relational) and vertical (religious) sins. Idolatry appears alongside greed and drunkenness, reflecting the specific temptations of Corinthian culture where civic life was inseparable from pagan worship.

Fee argues that Paul’s instruction creates the fundamental boundary between church and world: the church exercises internal discipline but leaves external judgment to God. This principle will be directly violated in 6:1–8, where believers drag each other before pagan courts.

Thiselton notes that Paul’s correction of the misunderstood letter demonstrates his awareness that written communication is inherently ambiguous and requires ongoing clarification. The distinction between inside and outside judgment is sociological as much as theological: the church functions as a bounded community with its own internal norms.

Thiselton observes that the Deuteronomy formula exarate ton ponēron ex hymōn autōn (‘expel the wicked person from among you’) is a performative utterance: by quoting it, Paul authorises the community to act. The OT formula becomes a NT ecclesial speech-act.

hist.context: Paul clarifies a misunderstanding from an earlier (now lost) letter (v. 9): when he told them not to associate with sexually immoral people, he did not mean pagans generally — that would require leaving the world entirely. He meant believers who persist in flagrant sin while claiming the name of Christ. The vice list in v. 11 (sexually immoral, greedy, idolaters, slanderers, drunkards, swindlers) is not exhaustive but representative of the moral failures that warrant communal discipline. The closing quotation from Deuteronomy (‘Expel the wicked person from among you’) connects the New Testament church’s practice to Israel’s covenant community discipline.

themes.note: Judgment and Holiness dominate: the chapter is Paul’s most concentrated teaching on church discipline, demanding the expulsion of the unrepentant offender. Justice rises with the call for communal accountability. Covenant scores highly through the Passover imagery and the Deuteronomy formula.

rows.label: vv.1–8
rows.text: The Incestuous Man: Judgment and the Passover Metaphor

lit.note: Chapter 5 transitions from the wisdom/divisions argument to specific ethical failures. The structure moves from a specific case (vv. 1–8) to a general principle (vv. 9–13), grounding church discipline in both Christology (Christ our Passover) and Torah (Deut 17:7).

hebtext.note: The noun porneia (sexual immorality) is a broad term encompassing all illicit sexual activity. In this case, the specific act is that a man ‘has his father’s wife’ (v. 1) — a euphemism for an ongoing sexual relationship with his stepmother. Such conduct was prohibited by both the Torah (Lev 18:8; Deut 22:30) and Roman law (the lex Iulia de adulteriis). Paul’s outrage is intensified by the fact that even Gentile moral standards (ethne) condemned such behaviour. The term porneia becomes a key word in the letter, recurring at 5:1; 6:13, 18; 7:2; 10:8.

The noun zymē (leaven/yeast) in vv. 6–8 draws on the Passover tradition of removing all leaven from the household (Exod 12:15). Paul deploys the metaphor: a small amount of yeast works through the whole batch of dough. Tolerating the incestuous man’s sin will corrupt the entire community. The command to ‘get rid of the old yeast’ (v. 7) and the identification of Christ as ‘our Passover lamb’ (to pascha hēmōn, v. 7) ground the ethical imperative in Christology: because Christ has been sacrificed, the community must live in purity.

The verb krinō (to judge) appears five times in vv. 12–13, creating a concentrated meditation on the proper scope of Christian judgment. Paul distinguishes between judging outsiders (which is God’s prerogative) and judging insiders (which is the church’s responsibility). Paul’s quotation of Deut 17:7 / 19:19 / 22:21 (‘Expel the wicked person from among you’) roots ecclesial discipline in Torah.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: 2): they may have interpreted their tolerance as evidence of spiritual maturity or ‘freedom’ in Christ…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Expel the Immoral Brother

translations.text: For my part, even though I am not physically present, I am with you in spirit. As one who is present with you in this way, I have already passed judgment in the name of our Lord Jesus on the one who has been doing this.
For though absent in body, I am present in spirit; and as if present, I have already pronounced judgment on the one who did such a thing.
And you are proud! Shouldn’t you rather have gone into mourning and have put out of your fellowship the man who has been doing this?
And you are arrogant! Ought you not rather to mourn? Let him who has done this be removed from among you.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Expel the Immoral Brother within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: The Torah explicitly prohibits uncovering the nakedness of one’s father’s wife — the offence Paul addresses here.
Jesus’ instructions on church discipline provide the dominical precedent for the communal judgment Paul commands.
Paul mentions handing Hymenaeus and Alexander over to Satan ‘to be taught not to blaspheme’ — a parallel remedial use of excommunication.
The direct source of Paul’s concluding command — the formula used in Israel’s covenant community to execute judgment on serious offenders.
Jesus’ instruction to treat the unrepentant sinner as ‘a pagan or a tax collector’ parallels Paul’s call for exclusion of the persistently immoral.
Paul instructs the Thessalonians to ‘take special note’ of the disobedient and not associate with them, while still warning them as brothers.

discourse.thesis: Sexual immorality in the community must be judged and expelled — Paul's pastoral authority extends to expelling the unrepentant because the church's holiness is its distinguishing mark before the world.

nodes.text: The presenting case: a man is living with his father's wife — a sexual immorality (πορνεία) not even found among pagans. And the Corinthians are arrogant (πεφυσιωμένοι — puffed up)! They should have mourned and removed (ἀρθῇ) this person from their fellowship.

Paul's apostolic judgment (though absent in body): deliver (παραδοῦναι) this man to Satan for the destruction of the flesh so that his spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord. Your boasting is not good — do you not know that a little leaven leavens the whole lump? Cleanse out the old leaven that you may be a new lump, unleavened as you are. For Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed.

children.text: The Passover metaphor: Israel removed leaven from their homes before Passover. The church is to remove moral corruption, since Christ (the true Passover lamb) has already been sacrificed. The ethical action flows from the theological reality — 'cleanse out... for Christ has been sacrificed.'

nodes.text: Clarification: Paul's earlier letter said not to associate with the sexually immoral — he did not mean the sexually immoral of the world (then you'd have to leave the world), but with any so-called brother who is guilty of these sins. Do not even eat with such a person. Those outside God will judge; remove the evil person from among you (Deut 17:7 — an OT purging formula applied to the church).

children.text: The distinction: judge insiders vs. leave outsiders to God. The church has no mandate to police the surrounding culture — it does have a mandate to maintain its own holiness. Confusing the two leads either to withdrawal from the world or to tolerance of sin within.

discourse.note: 1 Corinthians 5 addresses the church's failure to exercise discipline — which is itself a symptom of the wisdom-arrogance Paul has been diagnosing since chapter 1. The community's tolerance of sexual immorality is not open-mindedness but a failure of identity: they have forgotten that they are unleavened, because Christ the Passover lamb has been sacrificed.
